rules.check: New component APIs begin with flexible presentational components and behavior hooks unless the product pattern and its defaults are already stable.
rules.prefer: Build config components by composing established presentational parts and hooks instead of duplicating their behavior or markup.

rules.check: Content uses React children when consumers own the rendered elements or need free-form composition.
rules.prefer: Use data props when the component must own, transform, order, or constrain the rendered elements. Do not support equivalent children and data APIs simultaneously without a concrete need.

rules.check: Compound parts, render props, child-prop pass-through APIs, and escape hatches address demonstrated use cases rather than speculative flexibility.
rules.prefer: Start with the smallest API that supports known scenarios and open it up when a concrete need appears.

rules.check: Slots, useSlots, and __SLOT__ markers are only introduced when a parent must identify a specific child or the requested API requires child extraction.
rules.prefer: Use normal React composition and preserve consumer-authored child order.

rules.check: Components reuse existing base components, accessibility primitives, hooks, and utilities before introducing parallel implementations.
rules.prefer: Use primitives such as ButtonBase for Primer-owned native semantics, interactions, and reset styling.

rules.check: Components supporting controlled and uncontrolled state use the shared useControllableState pattern.
rules.prefer: Keep controlled value, default value, and change callback behavior aligned with the repository helper.

rules.check: Forwarded rest props and className are applied to the component root unless the public contract explicitly identifies another element.
rules.prefer: Test that forwarded props reach the documented element.

rules.check: New public callback props use a single object argument when the callback payload may grow.
rules.prefer: Use a named object payload instead of positional arguments.

rules.check: Render paths avoid browser-only APIs and hydration-dependent layout changes.
rules.prefer: Progressively enhance after hydration and move browser access into appropriate effects or event handlers.

rules.check: Focus moves as a direct result of user events rather than unrelated effect dependency changes.
rules.prefer: Manage focus in the event handler that caused the transition.

rules.check: Hooks that operate on a caller-owned DOM node accept a ref instead of creating and returning an incompatible ref.
rules.prefer: Let callers compose multiple behaviors around the same ref.

rules.check: Hook effects do not repeatedly resubscribe solely because a callback argument changed identity.
rules.prefer: Use the repository stable-callback pattern so subscriptions always call the latest callback.

rules.check: New public hooks represent a clear consumer capability rather than exposing subcomponent implementation details.
rules.prefer: Keep behavior hooks internal until a supported external use case and complete public contract exist.

rules.check: New components use a PascalCase directory and colocate implementation, CSS, tests, stories, docs metadata, and exports according to repository conventions.
rules.prefer: Name subcomponent files with the parent component prefix for discoverability.

rules.check: New shared modules that are not public API live under packages/react/src/internal and are not exported from public entrypoints.
rules.prefer: Keep implementation details internal until a supported public API is intentionally designed.

rules.check: New or changed component roots, public subcomponents, and meaningful structural parts follow the established data-component naming contract.
rules.prefer: Use PascalCase component API names, keep state in separate data attributes, and test stable identifier values.

rules.check: Public API changes update exports, export snapshots, docs metadata, tests, stories, and changesets as applicable.
rules.prefer: Treat all public surfaces as one component contract.

rules.check: The default story file uses the Components/ComponentName title and includes Default and Playground when configurable controls are supported.
rules.prefer: Keep the primary documentation stories in ComponentName.stories.tsx.

rules.check: Feature coverage and scenario examples are separated from the default story file.
rules.prefer: Use ComponentName.features.stories.tsx for features and ComponentName.examples.stories.tsx for distinct scenarios.

rules.check: Stories demonstrate supported props, variants, and composition rather than inventing undocumented APIs.
rules.prefer: Keep stories aligned with component docs metadata and real consumer-facing behavior.

rules.check: Stories do not add play functions as a substitute for interaction coverage.
rules.prefer: Exercise browser interactions through Playwright against the story and keep unit-level interactions in focused tests.

rules.check: Unit tests assert observable semantics and behavior from the user perspective rather than implementation details.
rules.prefer: Use Testing Library roles, accessible names, userEvent, and explicit contract assertions.

rules.check: Tests do not add catch-all snapshots of rendered React trees.
rules.prefer: Reserve snapshots for static objects with a clear review signal, such as package export lists.

rules.check: Behavior requiring real layout, browser interaction, visual regression, or automated accessibility coverage is tested through the Playwright e2e suite.
rules.prefer: Use @vrt and @avt coverage where visual appearance or accessibility output changes.

rules.check: New public props, forwarded DOM props, stable identifiers, keyboard behavior, and accessibility semantics have focused regression coverage.
rules.prefer: Name tests for the contract they protect.

rules.check: Component styles use colocated CSS Modules and Primer primitive custom properties.
rules.prefer: Avoid new runtime-injected component styling and hardcoded design values.

rules.check: A consumer className is merged with clsx on the top-level element and cannot be overwritten by prop spread order.
rules.prefer: Use CSS custom properties instead of multiple child className escape hatches.

rules.check: Component variants and state use data attributes or native pseudo-classes rather than modifier class names.
rules.prefer: Use :where() around data-attribute selectors to keep specificity low.

rules.check: Dynamic values crossing from JavaScript into CSS use custom properties instead of authored visual styles inline.
rules.prefer: Keep layout and visual rules in the CSS Module.

rules.check: Primer primitive custom properties are used without manually authored fallback values.
rules.prefer: Rely on the primitive build output to provide supported fallbacks.

rules.check: CSS newer than Baseline 2022 includes an appropriate fallback and broadly matching selectors avoid expensive :has() invalidation.
rules.prefer: Scope necessary :has() usage to a CSS Module class with a justified local stylelint disable.

rules.check: Deprecations update JSDoc, docs metadata, migration guidance, and the recommended replacement where applicable.
rules.prefer: Keep runtime behavior unchanged unless the change explicitly includes migration behavior.

rules.check: The changeset bump matches the largest consumer-facing API, behavior, CSS, accessibility, or stable-identifier impact.
rules.prefer: Use the repository versioning table and describe the outcome from the consumer perspective.

rules.check: Published runtime or public API changes include an accurate changeset; docs-only, test-only, story-only, and infrastructure-only changes do not.
rules.prefer: Keep each changeset terse and scoped to one consumer-facing change.
